key_insight: Models can turn their own test-time reasoning traces into persistent latent memories, but only when the experience is distilled into learned representations rather than reused as raw text.
engineering_relevance: This suggests a practical path to continual improvement in deployed LLMs: lightweight online adaptation that preserves useful reasoning patterns with tiny memory overhead instead of full retraining.
method: Use self-generated test-time signals, especially majority voting, as rewards for per-instance online learning.
Distill encountered reasoning into compact modular soft-prompt latent memories.
Retrieve the appropriate memory for future inputs to enable continual learning while reducing forgetting.
evidence_signal: Across challenging mathematical reasoning benchmarks, the method outperforms zero-shot and raw-data in-context learning baselines, and often matches or exceeds full offline training despite using only a few gradient steps and ~0.001% of model parameters.
watch_item: Performance depends on reliable self-generated reward signals and careful memory modularization; noisy signals or poor retrieval could limit transfer and stability.
paper_title: Continual Self-Improvement with Lightweight Experiential Latent Memories
